speaker: interviewer
text: Frances Flumenbaum. Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. That was in Sosnowiec. This was the last ghetto before they made judenrein. This means to clean up the Jews. And we all had to go out on a certain big place, of course. The young people were on one side, the older one on the other side, mothers and children on their arms, little children next to them holding their mom. Separations and separations and screaming and crying and prayers, always prayers. And when I looked at-- I looked it up all the way because I knew my parents I won't find anymore. So I was concentrating who else is not here or I can replace and do something for them. And I saw a older men and a mother and a little child on her arm and a German guard start-- start to separate them. And the father stretched his hand, and he said, give me the child. I know where I'm going. And I'm going to take him with me. You will survive.

speaker: interviewer
text: And there was another incident where the mother didn't want to give the child. So I walked over to the Jewish [? juden elder. ?] [INAUDIBLE] was his name. And I said, would you mind to let me go in her place and let her go with that child? He gave me a tiny slap, was not bad at all. And he said, you stay in line until you go and be called and separated. You don't have the right to move. Otherwise, you can wind up on the other side. And you know what it means. I didn't care what the other side meant. I didn't care where I was going to wind up.

speaker: interviewee
text: And several times I tried to replace somebody who was holding a child or the father or the mother. It didn't work. And after the selection-- this took days, the selection took days-- they took me and my sister to a camp. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And one camp I was a short time. This was--

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get there?

speaker: interviewee
text: By train. We got there by train, separated. They told us to keep everything what we have. I had my suitcase from home. And I wound up in Ottmuth. And this is a city somewhere in Germany. I don't remember where in. And again, I cannot call this lucky because I don't call lucky being alive at all, but I wasn't punished like others. The two camps that I went to were-- to compare-- like beautiful places to be according to the others, what my friends, my husband included, went through.

speaker: interviewer
text: Frances, before we talk about the camps, how was your ride on the train?

speaker: interviewee
text: The ride on the train was-- was pretty bad because we were stuffed in like animals, without water, without anything, till we arrived.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long?

speaker: interviewee
text: This took a couple hours, maybe four or five hours, something like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did anything happen on the train that you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: On the train, I remember people wanted to jump out the windows, to open, but they couldn't. And screaming and crying, where's my child? Where's my father? Where's my mother? Little ones, older ones, we all were 15 or 16 or were 14 years old. One girl, 12-year-old from my city. And that's all that I remember, crying and screaming. And we came to Ottmuth. And I got a job in a factory.

speaker: interviewer
text: Describe what you saw when the train opened.

speaker: interviewee
text: When the train opened, the Germans with dogs and [SIGHS] the pistols and all the guns, what they had in front, and screaming-- unnecessary screaming but everybody followed anyway. And threatening, and we should stay straight in line and just obey every little thing what they said. Some people, they couldn't even understand what they were saying. But we tried to help each other, the ones who knew each other. We stayed together.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was your sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: My sister was, at that time, still in ghetto. Yeah, she was-- I met her in another camp. And when we came to Ottmuth, they took us, of course, to a-- a concentration camp, which was a mild concentration camp. What I mean by that is like they didn't cut their hairs. I could wear whatever I had, my own clothes. And we had a little bit of water every day to keep ourselves clean as much as we could. The facilities wasn't that bad.

speaker: interviewer
text: Describe. I had a bed with a cover, to cover myself when it was cold. There was a oven to put some wood and to make a little bit warm in the winter time. They gave us a chance in the morning to wash ourselves, put clean clothes on, stay in the line, and march into the factory. They gave us breakfast, of course, first. What kind?

speaker: interviewee
text: The breakfast-- well, this was a first time breakfast in a camp-- was a poor-- very poor breakfast because when they gave you a little bit of soup was [INAUDIBLE] and a slice of bread. But to compare later on in life in camps, [? is all this-- ?] I remember this that's a good breakfast. Then we all went to the factory. And I had the job in the factory where I put together the right parts of the upper shoes, the leather, the right and the left in order, to make a pair of shoe. I was very handy. And I was fast and very particular in working. So I happened to have a master who was very good to me, the one who delivered-- took away from me the work. And he brought me every day a sandwich. He didn't say anything. But when I arrived there was a sandwich on my plate where I was sitting and working. I didn't say, of course, I ate it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And life was going on for a while. It was good, the way how I was working. Till one day the shoemaker in our camp said to me, look at this. See, you're working in a place where you can help people here in camp. If you go and bring me those pieces of leather, I can put shoes together and make the people go not barefoot-- the ones who didn't have shoes. I said, why not? Maybe I had it in mind also, when I said so easy, why not. Because I told that German who brings me every day that sandwich he wouldn't mind I'll take it. But I took it in a way to take away nobody should see it.

speaker: interviewee
text: And every day almost I took some parts, and he put them together and he made shoes. It wasn't that strict. That's why I could do it. And the camp wasn't that such a strict camp either. Were men and women together.

speaker: interviewer
text: And one day I was in the room [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in the juden [INAUDIBLE], the Jewish one, and also two Germans who came unexpected for inspection came into me. And she said, you come outside. I came outside and she said to me, did you-- were-- were you stealing and giving to the shoemaker those parts of the leather? I said, yes, I was stealing. What am I going to say? Did he ask you? I said no. He never asked me anything to steal, never told me anything to do wrong. Whatever I did, I did on my own. So she gave me two slaps on the face, which I was happy she did and not the German people. And she said from tomorrow on you're not going back to work to that place. And they gave me sort of a rougher place to work with, outside more when it was raining, it wasn't nice. But I never regretted-- I did-- I did. I was happy the shoemaker wasn't punished. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And till the end it was another few months, when I was in that camp, and there they transferred us, separated us from the men. It became a men camp. And they took us all to Ludwigsdorf. In Ludwigsdorf, I met my sister. How did you get there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Again by train, even worse. Because it was later on. In the beginning it probably was a little bit milder.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember any incidents that happened on the train?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. People were fainting. Some people died in the train. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we more-- more treated like really from beasts-- beasts treating animals. And then Ludwigsdorf was not a great camp, not great for me. I have to again repeat what my husband is telling me about his camp and what I heard about others, including Auschwitz in particular, and so and so. Again, Ludwigsdorf was fair.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I could wear my clothes, no clothes from a camp. Cutting hair I saw a few times or beating up by punishment only. Of course, what was the punishment? Stealing a potato or bringing in something-- something from the outside.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I did not have to do that. Because when I was standing in line and the Jewish people to select them, what kind of work to give them, I was chosen by the German juden [INAUDIBLE] to be her maid. She liked the way how I kept myself clean. As a matter fact, they took me out from line. They asked me, how did you clean your shoes? And I said with spucken. And you-- you know what I mean? No.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. With--

speaker: interviewer
text: Spit?

speaker: interviewee
text: Spit. With spit. My shoes were always clean. I tried to do my best wherever I could and to help myself. I did-- didn't drink the water before I washed myself. And she picked me up hundreds of girls to be her maid. So working for her-- I didn't know what this means, hunger. And-- sort of-- I had better than the others.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was a typical day like? Working for her?

speaker: interviewee
text: What it was?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. She had, first of all, in the morning when I came in, I put her white collar on the uniform, pressed it for her, kept everything just so when she was going out to the appel. She had a little dog and to keep up the dog, feed him around the house, not hard work, but just particular work where she likes-- she liked it. And again, I was going on and on for a while till I started to do the wrong thing. She had vodka in her closet. Vodka was a drink what my father used to have Shabbat-- after Shabbat. And he always took a sip, and he always said to me, you just-- on the tongue you can taste it. And I don't know how I did. I took out that vodka. And I said, oh my God. My father always said, taste it. Because always I went back to things to see my father again and again and not thinking about the consequences. And when she came in she smelled it, talking to me. This I didn't know at that time, that you can find out the easy way without even checking the bottle. And she said, I cannot keep you anymore here. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And they transferred me to a kitchen, which was again not too bad, working with the kitchen. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I was stealing. I couldn't help not stealing, stealing and bringing food. I was working with a girlfriend who kept up her religion the way how I was before the war. During the war, I [INAUDIBLE] a little but. I didn't go on with the [NON-ENGLISH]. Did my prayers. But she did more than I did because she had her father, and she knew where her father is. And she said, every day I'm going to pray three times a day that my father survives. He did survive. And she used to say, I'll help you. I'm going to give you every day a bread. And you take that bread to your room, hide it. And the bread [INAUDIBLE] the day before take it out. And we're going to bring this to the [GERMAN], the-- where the sick people were. And we going to take a few slices with us and see who is really hungry to give them. And that's what I did a while with help from my girlfriend. We were throwing over the fence slices of bread. We did a lot of things what we shouldn't do. And later on, they didn't want any more Jewish-- they selected the Jewish girls from the-- the Christian kitchen. And they took them in-- in the Jewish kitchen only. Over there I did not work. They took me to a factory where they made ammunition. And this was very, very bad. There were three colors-- yellow, gray, and a dark, dark gray, almost like black. I was in the dark gray. My skin, everything was so dark, like from a different race. The only thing what we got-- that I remember was nice-- they gave us a glass of milk during the work. Otherwise we probably wouldn't survive. And I was working with that ammunition till liberation.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were you doing?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was weighing on a scale. I remember there was-- in front of me was a screen. And I was sitting beside that screen. And there was a small, tiny little scale. And I was weighing the powder. And if there was a tiny, tiny little bit more-- explode. So this had to be exactly. And there were chosen people for that. And I was the one to be chosen. And I was working there until the end.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many hours a day?

speaker: interviewee
text: Eight hours. Night shift and day shift.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you sleep?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the day in the-- in the camp. And the-- and when they had day shift, we slept overnight. Otherwise we slept during the day. During the day, you couldn't sleep because otherwise they-- they didn't work on the same shift. They shared the room. But we went through.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you make any friends?

speaker: interviewee
text: I made friends, yes. I made friends with the ones-- like the one who was sharing with me, the stealing sharing and the giving out the bread to the ones who need it more than we did. She remains a very, very close friend. She still lives in Florida. I remember when she survived with her father came to camp. And she survived with her brother. Then I have several more friends from my own town who are here. And of course my sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your sister was with you in this camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: My sister was in same camp in Ludwigsdorf. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see her?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I saw her every day. Every day. Because she was working in the same ammunition place.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did anybody try to escape?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I remember when the men-- when we came there were still the men at the other side. This I remember-- being shot, caught. I remember certain punishments. This punishment I also won't forget in my life. When we came in, there were men to that camp. There were still men at the other side. There were a fountain in the middle of the camp. And there was a young man, at that time probably was 14 , 15, he was chained under the fountain. And the-- that water was-- Yes, I knew him very well from back home.

speaker: interviewer
text: And [INAUDIBLE], and I said to my sister, look at who-- who is there. Why did they do it to him? And he was there till he died. And this was my first thing that I saw in that camp. Probably they kept us-- they kept us like to show what can happen to you if you don't obey. Later on I understood.

speaker: interviewee
text: My sister survived with me. And she went with a girlfriend, also from our hometown, to Vienna, to look up for her brother. She found out through the Red Cross that her brother was alive. She needed companionship. So my sister said, I'll go [? with you. ?] You stay here in Ludwigsdorf. Remain in Ludwigsdorf. For months, I was still at the same camp. I didn't go out from camp. I could go out. I could establish my own life, see what I want to do. But I wasn't ready for anything. I thought the camp and if I have water enough to keep myself clean and food, probably could stay there as long as they are going to let me. And we were liberated by the Russians.

speaker: interviewer
text: What day was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: 8 Of May, 1945.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: What happened? I remember, all of a sudden, we heard 'come out, come out,' you know, in Russian. And already were people from other camps close around who were, a few hours before, they were liberated, talking in Jewish mainly. Come out. Don't be afraid anymore. The Germans are gone. We are liberated. We are free.

speaker: interviewer
text: And still didn't trust. But there were days where I'm sure, not me only but other people too, didn't care anymore what's going to happen. Well, what's gonna happen? What's gonna happen? If it's happened you were being [INAUDIBLE] part of family-- parent, sister, brother. And if not going to happen, so not going to take my own life. And we went out. And the Russians were there in the liberation. How did you feel?

speaker: interviewee
text: I felt like-- not happy, not happy, not want it to happen without thinking. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Why's it happen? And what's happened? It took me a few days, not only me my friends-- we were surrounded in a group always. I was never alone. And we were thinking, let's see what's gonna happen now. Where we belong, to who we belong, and what we want to do? And the main question, why has it happen? What did? To who do you ask that question? I was thankful to God for only one thing, that I had my sister. And I looked at her and I said, well, that's what God wanted. And it took me, really, weeks. Not-- not that I couldn't put myself physically together, mentally. Physically, I looked good compared to the others who I saw at the door from other camps. And I'm talking about months later. But mentally, I couldn't put myself together and think about what I want to do. So the first think in my mind was I'll go back home. And I'll go to the cemetery where I knew my grandfather was buried in the-- during the war. No, my grandfather was buried shortly before the war-- the war broke out. That's right, the end of '38. My grandmother, as soon the war broke out. My grandmother, I remember, they saying she died from agony knowing that she lost already in the war, when the war broke out she lost [BACKGROUND NOISE] already grandsons-- two grandsons. And she died of that. And I did so. I went back to Poland. I closed my eyes when I passed by my house where I used to live. I didn't go out. And I didn't want to see anybody but the cemetery. I went to the cemetery. I couldn't find the grave of my grandmother. But my grandfather was next to the rabbi from our town. And that grave was not only not touched, it was like kept up, like you would have done it now. And I took a picture from the grave, not me, a Pole, and he gave me the picture. And I came back.

speaker: interviewer
text: Months later I went again. And walking around in my town, I was thinking, who do I want to see? Of course I wanted to see one friend, a Polish girl who went through school with me. She was very nice to me, very nice. She went along with helping me in school, even though I was missing days. And I couldn't go to school, of course, in a Shabbat and holidays. And I came into her house. She greeted me beautifully, her parents. And I remember her mother said, now whatever I will put on the table, can you have a glass of water and eat something in my house or is it still like it used to be before the war? In the first moment, I didn't answer. I did not know what I want. Do I want to follow? And I said, I think I'm going to eat and drink in your house. I took a glass of water. And I ate bread. She put something on the bread. I didn't ask what it was. And I spent the day with them. And going up to the train and on the train I regretted very much. Here I'm walking away from the grave of my grandfather who I knew so well. And what do I want to do now, turn away from them or continue? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I thought to myself, I said, I did a mistake. I won't do it again. I will continue doing the way how I was raised, for respect and love, not that I know-- not maybe I didn't even believe at that time. Because the question why was still in me. And why do I have to follow what-- what's happened like anybody else said? It could happen like this, so this means what? Frances, we have to stop now.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.
